opp.ais_why:
• On the 73-minute 2/2/2026 demo, Yufeng Chen drove the AI conversation himself. Direct quotes: "Is your AI be able to do this?", "Is that an executable task for the agents?", and "how much of your percentage of revenue spend on AI development?" The note tagged as key takeaway is that he wants deeper insights into underlying algorithms and control mechanisms for a follow-up detailed demo.
• Yufeng pushed on Anna autonomous negotiation algorithms and guardrails, specifically how style is controlled and corporate reputation maintained. He also committed to share 4,400 lines of Amazon Business transaction data under NDA so Zycus could process it within a week and demo classification accuracy. That is buyer-funded, buyer-initiated POC behavior.
• Concrete next steps reinforce hunger: Zycus to send a mapping of platform names to each requirement tab, an AI agent capabilities slide, iSave plus Anna negotiation agent details, and an ESG scope and timeline assessment. Yufeng is asking for AI capability mapping, controls, and ROI: classic AI Hungry champion behavior.

items.area: IT position and specific integration concerns (Bob King, Pete)

items.quote: the VP of SC wants a vendor-led implementation
items.source: Next Step 24 Jun 2026; SF Contact Roles

metrics.narrative: S&C articulated clear operational pain around manual sourcing, supplier onboarding, 3DX/PLM drawing handling ('last week it took me an hour to put a package together, there's 14 drawings', Dan Chao 24 Jun) and lack of supplier-inventory visibility, but has quantified none of it. On 30 Jun Yufeng Chen asked for the price ('we do wanna know the dollar in our head, so I can plug that number in leadership's mind') yet no ROI, savings, headcount or cycle-time figure was ever attached. Without a quantified case the Economic Buyer (David Single, VP Finance) has no financial anchor to defend the $340K, so building the value model is a ranked move.

champion.narrative: Two strong, active champions. Yufeng Chen (Global Sourcing Manager) drives the technical evaluation, repeatedly reassures Zycus it is competitive despite the direct-materials gaps ('you guys are still in a very good shape', 24 Jun; 'none of those will be treated as a deal breaker', 24 Jun) and praises Zycus's candour as partnership behaviour. Katie Booth (Global Indirect Sourcing Manager - IT) confirmed Zycus is vendor of choice going into the final demo and openly downgrades rivals (iValua 'only because shortlisted last initiative', Oracle 'at IT's insistence'). Dan Chao (Senior Program Manager) actively coordinates scheduling and SME logistics. What is developed: technical fit, honesty-based trust, VOC status. What is missing: neither champion has demonstrated access to David Single or Jim Boss, so their advocacy stops at the manager layer and must be extended to the power layer.

competition.narrative: Zycus is the front-runner on a shortlist of three. Oracle is the ERP incumbent kept in 'at IT's insistence' though the business side is unhappy with it, and iValua is in 'only because they were shortlisted last initiative' (Next Step 24 Jun, Katie Booth) - neither is buyer-preferred. Coupa appears only in the Competitors field and was grouped with Zycus as an indirect-only player by the relevant stakeholder (24 Jun). The real threat is not a rival SRM suite but IT's Oracle preference plus the direct-materials/3DX-PLM capability gap where dedicated direct-sourcing peers (Jaggaer, Ivalua) hold native PLM connectors Zycus lacks. Zycus wins on vendor-led implementation (Oracle/iValua won't offer it), honesty-built trust, indirect SRM fit, and the Merlin/Anna AI story.

identify_pain.narrative: Confirmed pain: manual, fragmented sourcing and supplier management, painful engineering-drawing handling across PLM/3DX ('it took me an hour to put a package together, there's 14 drawings', Dan Chao 24 Jun), no supplier-side inventory visibility (VMI), and dissatisfaction with the Oracle status quo ('no one is happy with Oracle as a partner', Next Step 24 Jun). The pain owners are the sourcing organisation (Yufeng Chen, Global Sourcing Manager) and indirect/IT sourcing (Katie Booth). Inferred pain: growing direct-materials complexity ('a lot of BABA activity going on', Yufeng 30 Jun) that the buyer is deliberately keeping out of Phase 1 scope. The pain is real and articulated, but never converted into cost or time figures.

sources: Ronit Wadhwa / Katie Booth call 24 Jun 2026
SF Contact Roles (Harman Toor, Anthony Sitko)
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are surfacing but not yet active. Ronit Wadhwa (Zycus) referenced working 'on an MSA and SOW with Rick' (24 Jun) and the buyer expects custom 3DX/PLM work as a separate quoted line item scoped at SOW time ('that can come at SOW time when we're contracting and finalizing that implementation', Katie 24 Jun). CLM is integration-only via the relevant stakeholder ('the integration piece to the relevant stakeholder is what we're looking for', Katie 30 Jun). Legal contacts exist in Salesforce (Harman Toor, Commercial Corporate Counsel; Anthony Sitko, General Counsel) but have not engaged, and InfoSec/IT deep-dive is pending. Current contract expiration is not on record. Visibility improves once the IT deep-dive and SOW scoping begin.

sources: SF Contact Roles (David Single, Jim Boss, Kara Weiner)
economic_buyer.narrative: The economic layer is mapped in Salesforce but not engaged. David Single (Vice President Finance) is the budget owner and Jim Boss (Vice President, Global Operational Excellence) is the Executive Sponsor per Contact Roles, and Next Step (24 Jun) names the VP of Supply Chain (Kara Weiner) as wanting a vendor-led implementation. None of the three has appeared on any of the 13+ recorded buyer calls from Feb to Jun 2026; every commercial conversation has run through managers Yufeng Chen and Katie Booth. Pricing is entering the conversation (Yufeng 30 Jun) with no EB in the room, so engineering a Single/Boss value review before finalist scoring is the highest-leverage power move.

decision_process.narrative: The process is readable from the call sequence and Next Step. Zycus clarifies the three gap items (VMI, 3DX/PLM, OTM), the buyer confirms must-have vs nice-to-have in writing, then module demos run against the SOW tabs with ~35 SMEs scoring, feeding a final RFP submission on 18 Jul and a finalist decision. Dan Chao coordinates scheduling; Yufeng Chen and Katie Booth own scoring alignment. A formal IT integration deep-dive with lead Pete (and Bob King's approval) is a gate before contracting, and Next Step flags IT as 'a wild card' keeping Oracle alive. The approval chain runs manager champions -> VP Supply Chain / VP Finance / VP Ops -> IT sign-off -> legal.

decision_criteria.narrative: The criteria are explicit and documented in the 217-line RFP (Exhibit 1 Business Requirements SOW), scored line-by-line by module-specific SME panels: 'they provide a drop down score for each deliverable... ordered 1 2 3 4 5 just like in the SOW' (Katie Booth 30 Jun). The buyer weighs capability out-of-box vs configurable vs custom across ~9 functional tabs (supplier information management, performance/risk management, supplier development, collaboration, eProcurement, CLM). Two criteria carry decisive weight: 3DX/PLM integration for engineering-drawing handling ('3DX is so critical for us', Yufeng 30 Jun) and vendor-led implementation ('the executive Sponsor wants a vendor-led implementation, something Oracle and iValua won't do', Next Step 24 Jun). Direct-materials eProcurement has been de-scoped by the buyer as a scoring factor.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. It's genuinely competitive and could go either way; the reads below say where it stands.

blocks.how: A deal at 'Shortlisted' anchors near 55. Thin signals pull the read to 53.
blocks.title: Zycus win position

bullets.full: Champion is driving it — Yufeng Chen (Global Sourcing Manager) and Katie Booth (Global Indirect Sourcing Manager - IT) are strong, active champions with multi-call engagement (8, 24, 30 Jun) who confirmed Zycus is vendor of choice, de-risked the 3DX/PLM and VMI gaps as line items rather than deal breakers, and push back on Oracle and iValua on Zycus's behalf. What has developed: technical fit, honesty-built trust, VOC status and demo momentum. What is missing: neither has shown access to Economic Buyer David Single or Executive Sponsor Jim Boss, so advocacy is powerful at the manager layer but cannot yet convert to budget sign-off. Kara Weiner (VP Supply Chain) is a senior advocate for vendor-led implementation per Next Step but has not appeared on a recorded call, so her engagement is unconfirmed.

cro_panel.header: S&C Electric Company — Shortlisted · $340K · closes 24 Jul

contributions.evidence: Stage is ahead of the engagement/evidence the sweep found.

commentary.deal_risk: Risk 21 is driven by Stage is ahead of the engagement/evidence the sweep found. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.win_position: Win 53 is carried by champion strength +1.00 (weight 15); exec access strength +0.30 (weight 15). Offsetting it: momentum 51 vs stage-expected 56.
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 51 (roughly flat): Next Step carries 6 dated milestone(s) — actively worked. Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Early Read: 3 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (stage integrity, exec and champion, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 35 is anchored on the stage win (53), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 51, and dragged by risk 21. Read is Early Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.

contributions.evidence: Verdict flags close-date risk this sweep.

The deal is progressing fast at the working layer while the executive layer stays absent.

reasons.detail: The demo agenda mapping each RFP tab to SME sessions was our deliverable due Jul 1 and is still outstanding as of Jul 3 on a $340K Upside Key Deal at Shortlisted/finalist stage closing Jul 24. It is our lapse - agenda content is our internal-alignment output, not buyer-blocked. The final demo is the decisive VOC event; if it isn't sent to Dan Chao we cannot lock the 6-17 Jul dates, compressing or slipping the finalist demo and ceding momentum to Oracle, which survives only on IT's insistence.
reasons.metric: demo agenda 2 days overdue (due Jul 1, still open Jul 3)
reasons.ceo_ask: Ask Richard why the finalist demo agenda to Dan Chao is 2 days past due and whether the 6-17 Jul demo dates are locked before scoring.
reasons.summary: The demo agenda WE owe the buyer is past due as finalist scoring approaches.
reasons.evidence: our_open_deliverables: 'Share the demo agenda after an internal alignment call, keeping eProcurement at the far end' due 2026-07-01, status 'overdue'; recommended move still open: 'Send Dan Chao the module-by-module demo agenda... and lock 6-17 Jul dates'.

critical_signals.text: On the 30 June response call Zycus confirmed it will build the 3DX/PLM app and deliver supplier-inventory VMI visibility, and the buyer moved straight into scheduling the module demos, keeping Zycus as vendor of choice.

Oracle survives on the shortlist only because IT insists on it while the business side is openly unhappy, and iValua is in by inertia, so no rival is buyer-preferred but IT's Oracle lean is the live threat.
Zycus brought AVP Hemant into implementation planning, yet the buyer's economic layer, David Single in Finance and Jim Boss in Operations, has still never joined a single call.
Yufeng Chen asked for the price to take to leadership on 30 June, but no quantified savings or ROI case yet exists to justify the $340K to the VP of Finance.
The buyer narrowed the decisive scope to 3DX/PLM artifact handling and OTM inbound-PO integration and de-scoped direct-materials eProcurement as a scoring factor.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is strongly live and buyer-pulled: the customer requests meetings, names dates, is entering pricing and has confirmed vendor-of-choice status. The only cooling area is the untouched executive layer, not the working relationship.

north_star_verdict.math: 21 days to close (24 Jul from 3 Jul). Stage Shortlisted since 15 Mar 2026 (~110 days). CloseDate has slipped five times (26 Jun -> 24 Jul on 18 Jun; prior 15 May, 30 Mar chain). Remaining path: propose demo agenda, run 35-SME module demos (6-17 Jul window, NOT yet booked), final RFP submission 18 Jul, line-by-line scoring, finalist decision, EB/Exec-Sponsor briefing, IT integration deep-dive, legal/InfoSec, MSA/SOW. Dan Chao (24 Jun): 'originally hoping to close demos this week, next week, unrealistic at this point.' Realistic completion is 4-6 weeks, pushing close into late Aug. Timing is the sole real problem; the deal itself is live and favoured.
evidence: Zycus is vendor of choice going into the final demo (Next Step 24 Jun, Katie Booth). Buyer engagement is intense and current: response call 30 Jun, requirements discussion 24 Jun, requirements review 8 Jun, plus near-daily email threads through 2 Jul. This is a live, favoured deal, not a stalled one.

The close date is not credible. Module demos across ~9 RFP tabs with ~35 SMEs are not yet scheduled (Dan Chao, 24 Jun: demos this/next week are 'unrealistic'), final RFP is due 18 Jul, and finalist decision, IT deep-dive, legal and contracting all follow. The 24 Jul date will slip to late Aug.

Economic Buyer David Single (VP Finance) and Executive Sponsor Jim Boss (VP Global Operational Excellence) have not appeared on any recorded call. Champions Yufeng Chen and Katie Booth advocate strongly at the manager layer but cannot convert advocacy to budget sign-off without a direct Single/Boss briefing. Source: Avoma attendance, SF Contact Roles.

IT is the live competitive lever: Oracle stays on the shortlist 'at IT's insistence' (Next Step 24 Jun) while the business side is unhappy with it. CIO Bob King and IT lead Pete gate integration approval and the IT deep-dive is a formal step before contracting.

No quantified business case exists. Across recent calls the buyer articulated operational pain and asked for pricing ('we do wanna know the dollar in our head so I can plug that number in leadership's mind', Yufeng 30 Jun) but no ROI, savings, or cycle-time figure anchors the $340K for VP Finance.
north_star_verdict.headline: Healthy, deeply engaged deal where Zycus is vendor of choice, but the 24 Jul date will slip: module demos are still unscheduled, final RFP is due 18 Jul, and the economic layer (David Single, Jim Boss) has never joined a call.

flags: Multi-thread to the power layer: David Single (VP Finance) and Jim Boss (VP Ops) have never joined a call; engineer an executive value review before the 18 Jul final RFP so champions can convert advocacy to budget sign-off.
Neutralise IT's Oracle preference proactively: schedule the integration deep-dive with Bob King and Pete on 3DX/PLM and Oracle EBS/OTM, since IT is the only reason a disliked Oracle survives.
Reframe scoring away from the direct-materials/eProcurement gap toward the vendor-led implementation and Merlin/Anna AI wedge that Oracle and iValua cannot match.
best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy levers for a favoured Shortlisted deal whose only structural weakness is an unengaged power layer and an IT-held Oracle lever.

competitive_position.summary: Zycus is the front-runner (vendor of choice, Katie Booth 24 Jun) on a three-vendor shortlist. Ranked by CURRENT threat: Oracle is the strongest live threat, not because the business wants it but because IT (CIO Bob King, lead Pete) keeps it in 'at IT's insistence' and gates integration approval, while the business is openly unhappy with it. iValua is a low, inertia-only threat ('in only because shortlisted last initiative'). Coupa is dormant, named only in the CRM field and grouped with Zycus as an indirect-only player. The deeper competitive exposure is the 3DX/PLM native-connector gap where direct-sourcing specialists (Jaggaer, Ivalua) are stronger, but the buyer has de-scoped direct materials. Win path: neutralise IT's Oracle lean via the integration deep-dive, lead with vendor-led implementation and Merlin AI, and get the value case in front of Single/Boss before finalist scoring.
